topic: Performance - Avoiding Clone in Hot Paths

turns.content: I've read about the performance pitfalls of cloning large structs in Rust, but I still don't fully understand how to avoid it in a hot path. Can you explain why this is such a common mistake?

The core reason is that cloning creates a full copy on the heap, which triggers allocation and zeroing overhead every time you call your function. Think of it like copying an entire library bookshelf to a new room when you only needed to read one chapter; borrowing a specific page avoids moving all the books. In Rust code, this means your function body runs in a tight loop where cloning inside makes performance unacceptable.
That analogy makes sense. But what about when I actually need to modify a value? Should I just use Cow?

You should consider using std::borrow::Cow for conditional ownership, but be careful not to misuse it as a blanket solution. If you think cloning is always bad and blindly use Cow everywhere, you might end up paying unnecessary allocation costs when immutable borrowing would suffice. The key distinction is that Cow only helps when the data actually needs to change; otherwise simple references are better.
I see. So I should just avoid Clone? What about using indices instead of pointers for large collections?

Yes, avoiding Clone is the primary goal in hot paths, and using indices to track positions within a large collection like Vec or array avoids pointer indirection overhead entirely. This approach reduces memory pressure and cache misses significantly compared to managing pointers that require dereferencing.

topic: Performance - Avoiding Clone in Hot Paths

turns.content: I've read about the borrow checker but I still don't get how to avoid cloning when processing large arrays in a hot path. My Java code just cloness everything because it's safer, right? But here in Rust I'm seeing massive allocations and I need to understand why cloning is actually forbidden in performance-critical paths.

You're absolutely right about safety being easier with clones in other languages, but that cost is exactly what makes it a killer in hot paths. In Rust you can't clone an owned vector or slice without paying the full price of copying all elements. Instead you should borrow data using references like &mut Vec<u8> to avoid allocation entirely. If you need conditional ownership for read-only sections, use Cow instead of cloning every time. The key insight is that borrowing gives you temporary access without moving memory.
Okay so if I have a function iterating over a buffer and checking some flags, should I clone the whole thing or just borrow? But what if I need to modify part of it?

You're catching the exact edge case. If you only need read access, use &T borrowing via iterators like iter() or as_ref(). Only clone when absolutely necessary and that's rare in hot paths. For modifying parts without moving everything, consider using Cow<T> which lets you avoid cloning by returning an owned value only if mutation happens. This is the standard pattern for conditional ownership.

That makes sense but there's a specific gotcha I keep hitting - sometimes my code compiles but runs slower than expected even though I'm borrowing. Is it just about the borrow checker being too strict?

It's not the borrow checker being too strict; it's often that you're still doing unnecessary work or choosing wrong types like using pointers when indices would suffice. Pointers add indirection overhead while indices are direct and cheaper to compute. Also check if your loop body is actually executing multiple times per request - sometimes refactoring to avoid repeated cloning in the inner loop solves the performance issue without changing logic.
So I should be avoiding pointers entirely in favor of indices? And using Cow only when mutation happens?

Exactly. Use indices like usize or u32 instead of pointers for direct access patterns, and reserve Cow<T> specifically for cases where you might need to branch between immutable and mutable views. The pattern is: borrow via &mut or & for reads, use Cow for conditional writes, and avoid cloning unless the data size is trivial. This minimizes allocations in your critical path.
Wait so I shouldn't be using Clone at all? Even if it's just a few elements?

Clone isn't forbidden entirely but it should never be in a hot path where you process thousands of items per second. For small fixed-size structs or enums, cloning is fine because the cost is negligible compared to CPU cycles spent on logic. But for large arrays or vectors, even a single clone can add up to significant latency. The rule of thumb: avoid Clone unless profiling proves it's necessary.
Ah! So I should profile first and only use cloning when the data is small enough that the cost disappears? And rely on borrowing for everything else?

That's the aha moment. The strategy is simple: borrow whenever possible to avoid allocations, use Cow for conditional ownership without copying, and reserve Clone strictly for cases where it genuinely fits your performance budget. Once you internalize this flow - borrow > Cow > index-based access > minimal cloning only when necessary - your hot paths will run significantly faster.

topic: Performance - Avoiding Clone in Hot Paths

turns.content: I'm struggling with performance on my side project. I keep cloning Vec<serde_json::Value> everywhere and the app feels sluggish. Is there a better way to handle mutable data without copying?

You're hitting the Clone bound because you're trying to mutate owned data instead of borrowing it. Rust's borrow checker prevents this, but often developers don't realize they can use references or Cow for conditional ownership to avoid the clone cost entirely.
I thought I needed to clone if I want to modify the original later. Isn't that how it works? I just want to be sure I'm not missing something obvious about immutability.

You're assuming you must own everything, but Rust allows borrowing as long as lifetimes are clear. If your data can stay read-only in some paths and writable only in others, use Cow instead of cloning. That way you avoid the expensive allocation and copy that Clone performs on Vec.
That makes sense. So I should just swap clone calls with borrow or Cow? What about indices versus pointers for accessing elements inside a large collection?

Exactly, but be careful: using indices is faster than dereferencing pointers repeatedly in tight loops. If you're iterating over Vec and calling methods that internally clone or reallocate, refactor to use iterators with references instead of mutable copies.
